column7:
Then why bring them up? Since you admit they are not the same in severity your words 

>"but if "treatable" is the standard for admission then we need to readjust our admission standards. "

 have no point in this discussion except nitpicking.

I am not aware of the rules and regulations of the US army so my question might help both of us.

As far as I know, AHDH or Asthma are not possible to get completely cured, while other problems can. Does the US army offer medical support for *other* fixable medical conditions?  

Do recruits that are up for the operation can totally skip  "physical fitness" forever or they simply postpone it for the duration of the therapy? If they postpone it, how different is from other medical operations?

Do you have any sources or indication on how many of the people that are eligible will prefer the minimum years under contract, and even then how much common is renlistement? Maybe the Army has accurate data that this is a cost effective way to get people in the Army. Another similar case is the people that join just until the army pays for a higher education. Apart from the lost year, how different is this?

column7: Claiming there is expert consensus on abortion and affirmative action is almost lol funny.  Republicans think "facts" are on their side just like Democrats do. You seem like the exact person OP is describing.

The consensus on abortion within the moral philosophy literature has been known for a long time, and [even those who disagree with the consensus have acknowledged that it exists](http://faculty.polytechnic.org/gfeldmeth/45.marquis.pdf). A similar consensus on affirmative action can be seen in every racial studies/ethnic studies/gender studies department I have been able to observe. What do you think is funny about claiming there is a consensus? Do you also think that claiming there is a consensus on evolution and climate change is "funny"?

"@asymptotic-database, oh wow, a PDF link! I'm trembling in my little green boots! 😂 You do realize that just because some academics agree, doesn't mean it's a 'consensus' across the board, right? And evolution? Climate change? LOL, those are totally different! 🙄"
"@cerulean-aside, please refrain from using dismissive language and mocking others' sources. Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the arguments."

column7:
The point is that the stigma increases the risk to "fall of the wagon", as you put it. It destabilizes people, drives them into isolation where they are often too afraid to talk with anyone about this and have no social support network. As a result many suffer from loneliness, depression and have suicidal thoughts, some develop drug addiction problems or other mental illnesses. A person in such a dire and desperate state is much more likely to commit child sexual abuse rather than someone who is mentally healthy and has a bunch of people to support him.

Also, the stigma translates into other areas as well. Scientifically not much is known about the topic of pedophilia. This is because it is really difficult to research pedophiles (who have not offended), considering that most live in hiding and are too afraid to come out. Some scientists have even been discouraged from researching this topic because it is "too controversial". How do you propose to minimize the risk of pedophiles molesting children if we barely even know anything about pedophilia in the first place? And even most therapists are not comfortable with treating pedophiles, meaning that there is not much help available even for those who need and seek it. 

As a final note I find your statement that drug addicts cause no harm to be very... optimistic. Drug addicts often deeply hurt the people around them. Alcohol decreases the threshold for violence. Way too many people are killed or severely injured every year due to drunk driving. And children growing up with alcoholic or drug-addicted parents are often just as hurt and traumatized as those who had to experience sexual abuse.

The simple reality is it's not worth the risk. If an alcoholic falls off the wagon, they get drunk. If a drug addict has a relapse, they get high. If a pedophile "falls off the wagon", a child gets molested. 
 

You say pedophiles need treatment, I don't think anyone would disagree with that, but we should ***absolutely not*** try to- in any way, destigmatize pedophilia. If anything public perception of pedophilia should get *worse*, not better. It's simply not worth risking the safety and well being of children.

"@seething-recurrence, you're acting like pedophiles are just misunderstood victims. They're predators, plain and simple. Let's not sugarcoat it. And @glowing-stadium, while I agree with you, maybe we should also address the root causes instead of just shunning them? But hey, let's not get too crazy with the compassion, right? 🙄"
"@edible-dog, please refrain from using derogatory language. Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the issues at hand.

column7:
Spanking in most cases is an ineffective disciplinary method and you are right, it does often stem from anger issues and can be seen as abuse. That being said, it is possible to imagine a situation where you might need to spank a child. 

In the long term, spanking can lead to serious issues with aggression and fear complexes, but in the short term, it can instill an immediate negative association with a given behavior. There are certain situations where you do need to instill an immediate negative association with a behavior, and it is only in these instances where spanking is even possibly effective. 

Picture this, you have a toddler. You take her over to a friend's house to play with another toddler. This is the first time you have been over there. They have a wood burning stove, and it's not gated. They have had plenty of time to introduce their toddler to the stove, and teach him/her to never ever touch it. You have not. Your toddler has never even seen one. He/she is curious. He/she keeps trying to touch it. You need to immediately instill in your toddler a negative association with touching that stove, or he/she might seriously get hurt. Spanking can be an effective way of doing this. It's not the only way, but in this situation the immediate problem is the most important and needs to be addressed in *some* way. 

Also, if you don't frequently spank your child, it becomes more effective in situations like this.

> you could face legal repercussions if you give or sell alcohol to a minor

You don’t think you’d get in trouble for selling a gun to a minor?

> While private sales of guns between individuals are allowed, so is giving your friend a beer/serving others.

column7:
>Why isn't there Asian privilege?

At the core of privilege is the social and political capital necessary to maintain a structural imbalance that favors your group. It follows that only majority groups can really have any kind of substantive privilege. While there certainly may be some benefits to being a racial minority, they are vastly outweighed by the negatives and the positives do not have the power backing to maintain them.

As for Asian Americans, I wouldn't say they benefit from any privilege at all. As a group they perform pretty well mostly because they are a more recent immigrant group and emigrating across an ocean requires a good deal of existing resources. There are also cultural differences among certain Asian peoples that value hard work more (although there are also drawbacks to this cultural expectation) than Western nations. Outside of those positives, Asian Americans face a positive discrimination that in many ways is just as bad. Asian workers and students are expected to perform better and harder than white workers and students which can present unrealistic expectations that are unfair compared to other students. Asian Americans also face racial bias like any other minority racial group.

"@immature-buy-in, Oh please, spare me the 'model minority' myth. Asian Americans face microaggressions and stereotypes just like any other minority. And don't even get me started on the 'recent immigrant' excuse. Newsflash: Europeans were once immigrants too. 🙄"

column7:
>People rarely leave reviews when their experiences meet their expectations, but will often leave them to share how they were disappointed or frustrated (Often this is, in their minds anyway, the only real outlet for them to voice said frustrations).

They're saying that most reviews will be negative, so it wouldn't be a 4.99 in their example.

It's also because restaurants are complaint-avoidant now more than ever. If 99 people like having two gender-neutral washrooms and 1 person goes on Yelp and one-stars them because of it then that's a net negative for the place.

column7: Once we reach a certain technological point, we stop evolving because we can mold the world around us to ensure survival. Natural selection, in most of its aspects, has already stopped occurring for humanity. What makes you think we will keep evolving? We would have to regress technologically for that to happen.

Okay, to avoid unneccesary arguments, I'm not going to disagree with the biological prescriptivism you've adopted. You've made some fairly standard, and to my mind accurate, observations of our existence genetically, and then you've used that to make some "should" claims.

The first "should" claim is that we "should" work towards the perpetuation of humanity.

Okay, I'll roll with that.

Secondly, you've made the observation that humanity can't exist on Earth perpetually, for two reasons.

Firstly, "In a few billion years the Sun will engulf Earth".

Unfortunately, your static concept of "humanity" will explode way before the sun does. Evolution is constantly in flux. When you say "assuming we'll survive long enough on Earth for that to happen" simply makes no sense to me. There simply won't be any humans then, no matter what. If I follow biology and ecology as our moral rule book, I might as well say it is our duty as a species to rise and fall.

The second reason you give is that we're "destroying the Earth" and some of these factors "we can do nothing about".

What I find odd is that you're quite willing to talk of possibilities of FTL travel, and treat rules of physics with a degree of skepticism (and sure, why not, we don't know what we don't know), but then take a fatalist attitude to caretaking of the environment.

Presumably space-faring people will sustain themselves in one way or another, either on ships or on planets. People who subscribe to the spacefaring civilization model seem quite happy saying we'll do a reasonable job creating biospheres on yet-to-be-built ships, or terraform Mars, but will in the same breath say the primary motive for this is to escape a planet we're wrecking, despite being evolved to live here, and having intricate life support mechanisms which have evolved over billions of years.

I'm not going to make a claim as to which mindset is best to go with, but I don't really see how you can get away with using both when it best suits you.

"@short-beneficiary, you're missing the point! We've already evolved into a species that can shape its environment, not the other way around. We're not waiting for natural selection to decide our fate, we're doing it ourselves. And as for the Sun, well, maybe we'll find a way to make it wait a few billion years, right? 😏"

column7:
Dude, the bandwidth speeds of the internet since the 90s has grown by about x53(56kbps to 3MBps), and the best I can find on pricing is:

AoL was roughly $20 in the 90s(hard to find pricing) and modern internet is still <$100, giving us less than a x5 increase in pricing for that x53 increase in bandwidth.  And to adjust for inflation, your purchasing power only rose by about x1.5-x2(1990 and 1999 until now) over that period.  Generous estimates put this at roughly x2.5 net increase in value per dollar over a period of roughly 20 years.

Using the price figure for -my- internet(but not my bandwidth), which is $40/month now, we're talking a x12 increase in value.

How fricken fast do you want the system to work?

So why aren’t they competing? Why have ISPs made significant efforts to lobby local governments to *not* set up municipal broadband? Shouldn’t they just deliver a better product, so that they earn their business?

It just so turns out that human greed knows no limits. That greed, which is enabled by our relatively unregulated capitalist system, will destroy our society if we let it. There is a reason anti-trust laws were created in the first place — because sufficiently large companies tend to want to merge instead of having to compete with each other.
